payload.message:
Yes, probably.

The safe boundary is:

- `Do not` disable DSM’s global `User Home Service` yet.
- `Do` stop Synology Drive from watching/indexing `My Drive` home folders if you do not use them.

Why:
- Your live logs show `/volume1/homes/...` is coming from Synology Drive `My Drive`, not ShareSync.
- Your live ShareSync sessions on `edgesynology2` are only for:
  - `/volume1/mac`
  - `/volume1/mgmt`
  - `/volume1/Coldlion`
  - `/volume1/files`
  - `/volume1/Photography`
  - `/volume1/styleguides`
  - `/volume1/users`
- `My Drive` watches were added separately, for example `ahazan <-> /volume1/homes/ahazan` and `popdam <-> /volume1/homes/popdam`.

So if homes may be needed by some other package, the low-risk move is:
- leave `homes` itself alone
- disable Synology Drive `My Drive` for unused users, or globally if nobody uses it
- unlink stale Drive clients that only exist for personal/home sync

That should reduce Drive indexing/watch load without touching your valid NAS-to-NAS share sync set.

For the shared-root churn, the main issue is not that those roots are too broad. The issue is too many write-capable sync actors touching the same trees.

The practical way to reduce churn without hurting real business use is:

1. Keep ShareSync between the two NASes for the seven valid roots.
2. Minimize desktop Synology Drive client sync on those same roots, especially bidirectional sync.
3. Identify and unlink stale or unnecessary Drive clients first.
4. For users who need access, prefer:
- SMB/File Station/Web access for large shared creative folders
- selective sync for narrow working subfolders
- one-way backup tasks instead of two-way sync where possible
5. Stop personal `My Drive` watches if unused.
6. Exclude obviously useless churn sources where possible:
- recycle bins
- temp/swap files
- Adobe/cache/scratch-style folders if they are inside synced trees
7. On the worst hotspots like `styleguides/Disney`, find the specific device/account causing repeated rescans/conflicts and remove that client before changing the whole sync architecture.

The pattern from your logs supports that:
- ShareSync is syncing the seven valid roots NAS-to-NAS.
- Synology Drive is also watching those same roots locally.
- Additional `My Drive` watches exist for home folders.
- Historical personal/computer content inside valid shares is adding noise.
- The duplicate storms are most consistent with client-side sync conflicts layered on top of legitimate shared-root sync.

My recommendation:
- First disable Synology Drive `My Drive` for homes if you truly do not use it.
- Then audit and unlink stale desktop/mobile Drive clients.
- Only after that, if churn is still high, narrow specific client sync tasks on the hottest roots rather than changing the NAS-to-NAS ShareSync set.

If you want, I can do the next read-only pass to produce a concrete action list:
- which `My Drive` users are active on `edgesynology2`
- which Drive clients/devices are still connected
- which client/account appears to be driving the most churn in `styleguides`, `mac`, and `files`

payload.message:
Short version: yes, you can probably stop `My Drive` from consuming resources without touching the seven valid ShareSync roots.

What your logs show is:

- `homes` usage is coming from Synology Drive `My Drive`, not ShareSync.
- The `Disney` rescan storm I can prove right now is running inside ShareSync `session 26` for `/volume1/styleguides`, connected to `192.168.3.100` as `ahazan`, not clearly from a named employee laptop.

**1. Stop Synology Drive from watching/indexing My Drive home folders**
Lowest-risk path:

- In `Synology Drive Admin Console`, go to the area where `Team Folder` and `My Drive (home)` are managed.
- Disable `My Drive (home)` if nobody uses personal Drive space.
- If the UI only lets you do it per-user, disable it for all users you do not want.

Why I’m confident this is the right layer:
- Official Synology docs distinguish `My Drive (home)` from `homes`; `My Drive` is the personal home-backed Drive space, while `homes` is tied to DSM user home service. [Synology KB: My Drive vs homes](https://kb.synology.com/en-me/DSM/tutorial/Drive_difference_between_homes_My_Drive_home_folders)
- Official DSM docs show `homes` itself belongs to the DSM `User Home Service`, which is a separate system feature. [Synology KB: User Home Service](https://kb.synology.com/en-us/DSM/help/DSM/AdminCenter/file_user_advanced)

So:
- `Disable My Drive first`
- `Do not disable DSM User Home Service yet` unless you know no package still depends on it

**2. Disable Synology Drive My Drive for unused users**
Use the same Drive Admin Console `My Drive` management area and turn it off for users who should only use team folders.

From your own logs, `My Drive` was enabled at least for:
- `ahazan`
- `popdam`
- and historically domain users like `IML\MCameron`, `IML\adweck`, `IML\lherrera`, `IML\apinilla`, `IML\asilva`, `IML\eparkin`

So the next cleanup target is:
- disable `My Drive` for any of those who do not need personal Drive space
- unlink stale Drive clients for ex-employees or old devices

**3. Minimize desktop Synology Drive client sync for legitimate remote workers**
Do not sync the full seven shares to desktops unless there is a very specific reason.

Best-practice version for your environment:
- Give each remote worker only the smallest subfolder set they actively need.
- Avoid two-way sync on broad creative trees like large `styleguides` branches.
- Prefer SMB/VPN, remote desktop, or browser/File Station access for heavy shared asset libraries.
- Use one-way backup tasks when the workstation is a source archive, not a collaboration node.
- Avoid having multiple users sync the same live PSD/AI-heavy folder tree to local disks.
- If a user truly needs Drive Client, use selective sync down to project/customer/licensor subfolders, not whole departments.

For your setup, the high-risk pattern is:
- `ShareSync between NASes` plus `multiple desktop two-way Drive clients on the same live folders`

That combination creates exactly the churn you’re seeing.

**4. Exclude obviously useless churn sources**
You should blacklist temp/junk that should never traverse Drive.

High-value exclusions for your environment:
- Adobe temp files like `~ai-*.tmp`
- generic `*.tmp`, `*.temp`, `*.swp`, `*.lnk`
- macOS junk like `__MACOSX`
- working cache/scratch folders
- app-generated temp staging folders
- recycle-bin noise if that path is exposed through sync workflows

I can already prove some filtering is happening:
- your logs show `~ai-..._.tmp` files being blocked by suffix-rule filters on `2026-05-04`

So the next move is not “invent filtering,” it’s “expand and verify the blacklist.”

**5. Can I find the specific device/account causing repeated rescans/conflicts in `styleguides/Disney`?**
Partially, yes.

What I can prove right now:
- The `Disney` storm is in `view 5` / `styleguides`
- The repeated directory rescans are showing in `ShareSync` daemon logs, not just generic Drive-client logs
- On `2026-05-04`, ShareSync `session 26` was started for:
  - `share_name: "styleguides"`
  - `sync_folder: "/volume1/styleguides"`
- The active ShareSync connection is to:
  - `server_ip: 192.168.3.100`
  - `username: ahazan`
  - `computer_name: edgesynology2`

What I cannot prove from the current logs:
- a specific employee laptop name as the original source of the Disney edits
- whether the original churn started on `edgesynology1` directly, or on a workstation syncing into `edgesynology1`

So the strongest current attribution is:
- `Immediate actor`: ShareSync session 26 on `edgesynology2`
- `Remote peer`: `edgesynology1` at `192.168.3.100`
- `Unproven upstream origin`: a specific user device

That means the Disney storm is currently propagating at the NAS-to-NAS layer, even if a workstation originally caused it.

**Recommendation order**
1. Disable unused `My Drive` users first.
2. Keep the seven valid ShareSync roots unchanged.
3. Audit desktop Drive clients and remove broad two-way sync tasks on those roots.
4. Expand suffix/path exclusions.
5. Then do a deeper attribution pass on `edgesynology1` to find which workstation or sync task is feeding `styleguides/Disney`.

If you want, I can do the next read-only pass specifically on:
- `which users/devices still have My Drive active`
- `which blacklist rules are currently configured`
- `whether edgesynology1 logs expose the upstream workstation behind Disney`

Sources:
- [Synology KB: My Drive vs homes](https://kb.synology.com/en-me/DSM/tutorial/Drive_difference_between_homes_My_Drive_home_folders)
- [Synology KB: DSM User Home Service](https://kb.synology.com/en-us/DSM/help/DSM/AdminCenter/file_user_advanced)
- [Synology Drive specifications](https://www.synology.com/en-us/dsm/7.3/software_spec/synology_drive)

entries.note: kept this read-only and evidence-first per Synology investigation preference
used prior Synology investigation workflow context for edgesynology2-style troubleshooting
kept no-changes investigation baseline
used prior context that the two NASes participate in sync-related investigations

**1. Stop Synology Drive from watching/indexing My Drive home folders**
Lowest-risk path:

- In `Synology Drive Admin Console`, go to the area where `Team Folder` and `My Drive (home)` are managed.
- Disable `My Drive (home)` if nobody uses personal Drive space.
- If the UI only lets you do it per-user, disable it for all users you do not want.

Why I’m confident this is the right layer:
- Official Synology docs distinguish `My Drive (home)` from `homes`; `My Drive` is the personal home-backed Drive space, while `homes` is tied to DSM user home service. [Synology KB: My Drive vs homes](https://kb.synology.com/en-me/DSM/tutorial/Drive_difference_between_homes_My_Drive_home_folders)
- Official DSM docs show `homes` itself belongs to the DSM `User Home Service`, which is a separate system feature. [Synology KB: User Home Service](https://kb.synology.com/en-us/DSM/help/DSM/AdminCenter/file_user_advanced)

- `Disable My Drive first`
- `Do not disable DSM User Home Service yet` unless you know no package still depends on it

- `popdam`

### Review findings

- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

payload.message:
Yes. Based on what we’ve proven, `edgesynology2` is not using `homes` because of ShareSync. It is using `homes` because Synology Drive `My Drive` is enabled, and on Synology that may require `User Home Service`.

The important safety point is:

- Disabling `User Home Service` does **not** delete the `homes` folder. Synology’s user guide says the folder is kept and becomes admin-only.
- The real risk is package dependencies, not data deletion.

Official references:
- [Synology User Guide: when User Home Service is disabled, homes is kept and admin-only](https://global.download.synology.com/download/Document/Software/UserGuide/Firmware/DSM/3.1/enu/Syno_UsersGuide_NAServer_enu.pdf)
- [Synology KB: My Drive is backed by home/homes](https://kb.synology.com/en-me/DSM/tutorial/Drive_difference_between_homes_My_Drive_home_folders)
- [DSM User Home Service docs](https://kb.synology.com/en-us/DSM/help/DSM/AdminCenter/file_user_advanced)

What likely stopped you last time:
- a package that depends on `User Home Service`
- historically common ones are Synology Drive `My Drive`, Photos/Moments-style personal libraries, Mail-related packages, or anything using per-user storage

So the right approach is not “just turn it off.” It is:

1. Identify what on `edgesynology2` still depends on `homes`.
2. Disable `My Drive` usage first where possible.
3. Then disable `User Home Service` only after confirming no needed package still requires it.

For the `Disney` churn question, your clarification changes the conclusion:

- `192.168.3.100` is `edgesynology1`, and that ShareSync link is legitimate.
- So the immediate churn executor on `edgesynology2` is `ShareSync session 26` for `/volume1/styleguides`.
- To find the *upstream* source of the `Disney` storm, we now need to inspect `edgesynology1`, not `edgesynology2`.

That is the clean split now:
- `edgesynology2 homes` issue: likely safe to remove from resource usage, but only by dealing with `User Home Service` dependencies
- `styleguides/Disney` churn origin: likely upstream on `edgesynology1` or a client feeding `edgesynology1`

Best next step:
- I do a read-only dependency audit on `edgesynology2` to identify exactly what would break if `User Home Service` is disabled
- then I pivot to `edgesynology1` to attribute the upstream `Disney` churn source

If you want, I’ll do both in that order.

echo 'HOST:'; hostname
echo 'USERHOME/PKG STATUS:'
/usr/syno/bin/synoservicecfg --status pkgctl-SynoFinder pkgctl-SynologyDrive pkgctl-CloudSync pkgctl-SynoOffice pkgctl-ActiveBackup pkgctl-DirectoryServer 2>/dev/null || true
/usr/syno/bin/synopkg list --name 2>/dev/null || true
echo '--- DRIVE MYDRIVE LOG HITS ---'
grep -hE 'my-drive|/volume1/homes/|/var/services/homes|Add user.?.my-drive|Request clientd to watch for .*homes' /volume1/@synologydrive/log/syncfolder.log /volume1/@synologydrive/log/syncfolder.log_* 2>/dev/null | tail -200
'@; $script | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 1904 ahazan@192.168.3.101 bash -s","timeout_ms":600000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script = @'
echo 'HOST:'; hostname
echo 'DRIVE DISNEY HOTSPOTS:'
grep -hE '/Disney/|RepeatedDownloadReq|fake event|Conflict|NativeRemove|rescan-handler' /volume1/@synologydrive/log/client.log /volume1/@synologydrive/log/client.log_* 2>/dev/null | sed -n '1,1200p' > /tmp/disney_hits.txt
python3 - <<'PY'
from collections import Counter
import re
c=Counter()
paths=Counter()
for line in open('/tmp/disney_hits.txt','r',errors='ignore'):
    if '/Disney/' not in line: continue
    m=re.search(r'\[(\d+)\]\[/Disney/', line)
    if m: c['view_'+m.group(1)] += 1
    if 'rescan-handler' in line:
        m2=re.search(r"Rescan directory '(/Disney/[^']+)'", line)
        if m2: paths[m2.group(1).split('/')[2] if len(m2.group(1).split('/'))>2 else m2.group(1)] += 1
print('VIEWS')
for k,v in c.most_common(10): print(v,k)
print('TOP_RESCAN_SUBROOTS')
for k,v in paths.most_common(25): print(v,k)
PY

echo '--- POSSIBLE CLIENT/DEVICE HINTS ---'
grep -hEi 'computer_name|device|client|session|username|server_ip|conn_id|linked:' /volume1/@synologydrive/log/client.log /volume1/@synologydrive/log/client.log_* 2>/dev/null | tail -300
'@; $script | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 bash -s","timeout_ms":600000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

command:
$script = @'
echo 'HOST:'; hostname
echo 'PACKAGES:'
/usr/syno/bin/synopkg list 2>/dev/null || synopkg list 2>/dev/null || true
echo '--- HOMES/USERHOME HITS ---'
for p in /var/packages/*/conf /var/packages/*/etc /usr/syno/etc/packages/*; do
  [ -e "$p" ] || continue
  grep -RHiE 'user home|userhome|/volume1/homes|/var/services/homes|My Drive|my-drive' "$p" 2>/dev/null | sed -n '1,200p'
done
'@; $script | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 1904 ahazan@192.168.3.101 bash -s

HOST:
edgesynology2
PACKAGES:
OAuthService-1.2.0-0163: OAuth Service is an open standard protocol for account authorization and authentication. It allows resource owners to authorize third-party access to their server resources without sharing their credentials. The package adopts OAuth 2.0 standard.
QuickConnect-1.0.9-0171: QuickConnect allows client applications to connect to your Synology NAS via the Internet without setting up port forwarding rules. It can also work with Synology-developed packages, such as File Station, Surveillance Station, Synology Drive, Synology Photos, and mobile applications.
SynoFinder-1.9.0-0900: Synology Universal Search is a powerful search tool for your Synology NAS. In one search, you can find matched items from photos to notes. You can also index folders to provide precise and prompt search results by filename or file content. With Synology Universal Search, everything on your Synology NAS is just a few clicks away.
UniversalViewer-1.4.0-0712: Universal Viewer allows you to view different file formats directly on the Synology Drive without having to open each application. Certain packages require Universal Viewer to function fully.
FileStation-1.4.3-1609: File Station is the centralized file management tool for your Synology NAS. Through File Station, you can manage and search files/folders, view files of various types, share private files with external users, mount remote folders and virtual drives for access, and do much more!
HybridShare-1.5.1-1826: Hybrid Share is a cloud file management solution that combines C2 cloud storage with on-prem NAS hosts across multiple locations. It offers high scalability, quick multi-site file access, and rapid deployment, facilitating efficient cross-site collaboration.
SMBService-4.15.13-3047: SMB Service is an easy-to-use file service management tool, allowing you to manage general settings related to the SMB/CIFS file sharing service. Through SMB Service, Windows, macOS, and Linux users can access your Synology NAS from the network once you have enabled the SMB service option in Control Panel.
ContainerManager-24.0.2-1606: Container Manager is a lightweight virtualization application that gives you the ability to run thousands of containers created by developers from all over the world on DSM. The hugely popular built-in image repository, Docker Hub, allows you to find shared applications from other talented developers.
Git-2.39.1-1079: Git is an open source distributed version control system, which provides an effective way to maintain large projects. With Git, you can create multiple local branches that are entirely independent of each other to try out ideas for new features you are working on. By using Git, you can collaborate with different groups of people simultaneously with relative ease.
StorageManager-1.0.1-1100: Storage Manager is a built-in application for managing storage on your Synology NAS. It offers a centralized interface to allocate storage capacity, monitor usage, check your drives' health, and use advanced features such as hot spare drives and SSD cache.
SynoAnalytics-1.0.3-20013: 
StorageAnalyzer-2.1.1-0641: Storage Analyzer allows you to have a quick glance at the overall usage trends of your Synology NAS, create and manage tasks to analyze storage spaces, and generate detailed reports on volume usage.
ActiveInsight-3.0.5-24122: Active Insight is a cloud service that provides better technical support experience with faster troubleshooting. When enabled on multiple of your Synology hosts, it allows you to manage them across locations with a performance monitoring and automated warning system.
CloudSync-2.7.2-2714: With Cloud Sync, you can seamlessly sync and share files among your Synology NAS and multiple public clouds, such as Dropbox, Baidu Cloud and Google Drive.
SynologyApplicationService-1.8.2-20726: Synology Application Service provides a framework to enrich features and services of Synology packages, including push notifications and profile settings. With Synology Application Service, coherent configurations can be shared across different packages.
Python2-2.7.18-1004: Python is an interpreted, interactive, and object-oriented programming language suitable for distributed application development, scripting, numeric computing and system testing. With Python, you can develop or integrate your systems more quickly and effectively.
DhcpServer-1.0.2-0046: DHCP Server turns your DiskStation into a DHCP server within LAN to assign dynamic IP addresses and manage DHCP clients.
HyperBackup-4.2.1-4228: Hyper Backup helps you protect shared folder data, packages, and LUNs on your Synology storage system, providing diverse backup destinations and multiple backup versions. Restoration is available with a straightforward version-browsing interface.
ScsiTarget-1.0.12-0338: SAN Manager helps you to manage and monitor your iSCSI and Fibre Channel services easily. Both iSCSI and Fibre Channel are storage area network (SAN) services that provide access to consolidated and block-level data storage. Clients can access storage space over storage network as if the space were local disks.
Node.js_v20-20.19.5-1014: Built on Chrome's JavaScript runtime, Node.js has become a widely popular platform for users to easily build fast, scalable network applications.
SnapshotReplication-7.4.7-1859: Snapshot Replication is an expert of data backup and recovery with the point-in-time copy mechanism. Businesses need data protection to prevent data loss from accidental deletion, application crashes, data corruption, and viruses.
SynologyDrive-4.0.2-27889: Synology Drive Server is a comprehensive solution for file storage, management, sharing, and synchronization. It is equipped with Synology Drive Admin Console for centralized management and monitoring, Synology Drive for file access via web portal, and Synology Drive ShareSync for synchronization across Synology NAS devices.
Tailscale-1.58.2-700058002: Connect all your devices using WireGuard, without the hassle.
Node.js_v18-18.18.2-1011: Built on Chrome's JavaScript runtime, Node.js has become a widely popular platform for users to easily build fast, scalable network applications.
LogCenter-1.3.1-2016: Log Center offers an easy solution for gathering and displaying log messages from network devices. It provides you with a centralized log management interface and the flexible search function to help you find the useful information efficiently.
SecureSignIn-1.1.6-0391: Secure SignIn Service provides enhanced account security for your DSM. It allows passwordless logins and offers sign-in options in addition to basic 2-factor authentication methods.
ReplicationService-1.3.0-0600: Replication Service is a core service that offers the ability to replicate taken snapshots from the source to the destination. This package cannot run alone and depends on core packages that offer snapshot features.
SupportService-2.0.4-21066: 
SynoOnlinePack_v2-1050: This package is for users to update drive database online

echo HOST=$(hostname)

echo 'SHARESYNC SESSIONS'
grep -hE 'add_session|Folder .*/volume1/|computer_name:|server_ip:' /volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync/log/dscc.log /volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync/log/dscc.log_* 2>/dev/null | tail -n 200

payload.message:
Here’s the current readout.

On `edgesynology2`, I did not find evidence that `homes` is part of ShareSync. The live issue remains Synology Drive `My Drive`/home-backed watching. I could not prove a second package dependency from config text alone in this pass. The only concrete package-level hit I got was File Station UI text explicitly warning that some functions require `User Home`. That means the real risk is still “package feature dependency,” not data loss.

The safest interpretation is:
- disabling `User Home Service` should not delete `homes`; Synology documents that the folder remains and becomes admin-only
- what may stop you is a package or feature that expects home-backed storage
- the most likely one in your environment is Synology Drive `My Drive`, not ShareSync

On `edgesynology1`, I got the more important churn result:
- `Disney` activity is overwhelmingly on `view 18`
- on `edgesynology1`, `view 18` is definitively `/volume1/styleguides`
- I counted `17,545` Disney log hits on that view
- I did not get a laptop/computer name from the logs yet
- but I did get strong proof that desktop-client style temp activity is hitting watched roots on `edgesynology1`, not just NAS-to-NAS ShareSync

The strongest proof is this:
- `view 18` logs show repeated scrubbing of `Unconfirmed *.crdownload` files inside `/volume1/styleguides`
- that pattern is browser-download temp file behavior
- ShareSync alone does not create Chrome `.crdownload` temp names
- so at least some of the churn is being introduced by human workstation activity inside the watched `styleguides` tree on `edgesynology1`

That is the clearest root-cause direction so far:
- `edgesynology1` is the upstream live source for the styleguides storm
- ShareSync to `edgesynology2` is legitimate, but it is propagating churn created upstream
- the upstream churn is not purely NAS-internal; desktop/client behavior is definitely involved

Two other useful details:
- on `edgesynology1`, the home-backed Drive watch is only `/volume1/homes/ahazan/Drive`, not the whole home tree
- on `edgesynology1`, the logs also show classic temp/rename churn in other watched roots like `Decor` and `SharedLic`, including `~ai-*.tmp`, `.tmp`, fake rename, fallback remove-and-upload, and “file not modified, this could be a fake event”

So the most practical next steps are:

1. For `homes`
Keep `User Home Service` on until we do one deliberate dependency check in DSM UI:
- Synology Drive `My Drive`
- any Photos/Mail/personal-storage features you still use
- anything obviously mapped to `home/homes`

2. For `styleguides`
The problem to solve is on `edgesynology1`, not the ShareSync link itself.

3. To reduce churn without harming legit work
Block or discourage these inside synced business roots:
- `.crdownload`
- `~ai-*.tmp`
- generic office/app temp `.tmp`
- browser-download staging inside watched folders

4. For attribution
The next read-only pass should be on `edgesynology1` specifically for:
- connected Synology Drive clients/devices if exposed by the admin DB/logs
- who is touching `/volume1/styleguides/Disney`
- whether the same user/device is generating the `.crdownload` scrubbing pattern

If you want, I’ll do the next pass focused only on `edgesynology1` and try to extract actual connected client/device identities behind `styleguides`.

Sources:
- [Synology KB: My Drive vs homes](https://kb.synology.com/en-me/DSM/tutorial/Drive_difference_between_homes_My_Drive_home_folders)
- [DSM User Home Service docs](https://kb.synology.com/en-us/DSM/help/DSM/AdminCenter/file_user_advanced)
- [Synology user guide note that disabling User Home keeps `homes` and makes it admin-only](https://global.download.synology.com/download/Document/Software/UserGuide/Firmware/DSM/3.1/enu/Syno_UsersGuide_NAServer_enu.pdf)

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message: The Drive admin DB has exactly what we need: `user_table` for views and `session_table`/`client_udc_table` for connected clients. I’m querying those joins now for `view 18` and any active sessions on `styleguides`.

content.text: The Drive admin DB has exactly what we need: `user_table` for views and `session_table`/`client_udc_table` for connected clients. I’m querying those joins now for `view 18` and any active sessions on `styleguides`.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script = @'
echo HOST=$(hostname)
echo 'VIEW 18 USER ROW'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/user-db.sqlite "select id,view_id,name,uid,gid,user_type,watch_path,share_uuid,enable_watch_home from user_table where view_id=18;"
echo 'STYLEGUIDES-LIKE USERS'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/user-db.sqlite "select id,view_id,name,uid,gid,user_type,watch_path from user_table where lower(name) like '%styleguide%' or lower(watch_path) like '%styleguide%';"
echo 'SESSIONS FOR VIEW 18 USER'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/user-db.sqlite "select u.view_id,u.name,u.watch_path,s.sess_id,s.client,s.ip,s.client_type,s.client_platform,s.client_version,s.device_uuid,s.ctime,s.last_auth_time from user_table u left join session_table s on s.uid=u.id where u.view_id=18 order by s.last_auth_time desc;"
echo 'ALL RECENT SESSIONS'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/user-db.sqlite "select u.view_id,u.name,s.sess_id,s.client,s.ip,s.client_platform,s.client_version,s.device_uuid,s.last_auth_time from session_table s join user_table u on u.id=s.uid order by s.last_auth_time desc limit 50;"
'@; $script | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 bash -s","timeout_ms":600000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

command:
$script = @'
echo HOST=$(hostname)
echo 'CLIENT UDC JOIN'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/user-db.sqlite "attach '/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/client-udc-db.sqlite' as udc; select u.view_id,u.name,s.sess_id,s.client,s.ip,s.client_platform,s.client_version,s.device_uuid,c.version as udc_version,c.region,c.os,c.two_way_sync_task_count,c.all_sync_task_count,c.enable_sync_temp_files_sync_task_count,c.enable_sync_dot_prefix_files_sync_task_count from user_table u join session_table s on s.uid=u.id left join udc.client_udc_table c on c.sess_id=s.sess_id where u.view_id=18 or lower(u.name) like '%styleguide%' order by s.last_auth_time desc;"
'@; $script | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 bash -s

payload.message: The key finding is that `styleguides` itself is not a logged-in “client account.” It is a watched shared root `@styleguides`, and the actual human devices are separate user sessions. I’m querying who is mapped to `view 18` next.

content.text: The key finding is that `styleguides` itself is not a logged-in “client account.” It is a watched shared root `@styleguides`, and the actual human devices are separate user sessions. I’m querying who is mapped to `view 18` next.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script = @'
echo HOST=$(hostname)
echo 'USERS MAPPED TO VIEW 18'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/view-route-db.sqlite "attach '/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/user-db.sqlite' as udb; select sv.shared_user_uid, count(*) as rows, ut.name as user_name, ut.view_id as user_view, ut.watch_path from share_view_table sv left join udb.user_table ut on ut.uid=sv.shared_user_uid where sv.view_id=18 group by sv.shared_user_uid, ut.name, ut.view_id, ut.watch_path order by rows desc limit 100;"
echo 'TARGET TYPES FOR VIEW 18'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/view-route-db.sqlite "select target_type, count(*) as rows from share_view_table where view_id=18 group by target_type order by rows desc;"
echo 'SAMPLE TARGET IDS VIEW 18'
sqlite3 -header -column /volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/view-route-db.sqlite "select shared_user_uid,target_id,target_type,view_id from share_view_table where view_id=18 limit 30;"
'@; $script | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 bash -s","timeout_ms":600000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$script = @'
echo HOST=$(hostname)
echo 'RECENT STYLEGUIDES-RELATED HUMANS'
grep -hEi '/Disney/|/WB/|/Star Wars/|/Aardman Animations/|/One Piece/|view 18' /volume1/@synologydrive/log/client.log /volume1/@synologydrive/log/client.log_* 2>/dev/null > /tmp/view18.log
python3 - <<'PY'
import re,collections
threads=collections.Counter()
with open('/tmp/view18.log','r',errors='ignore') as f:
    for line in f:
        m=re.search(r'\((\d+):(\s*\d+)\)', line)
        if m: threads[m.group(1)+':'+m.group(2).strip()] += 1
for k,v in threads.most_common(20):
    print(v,k)
PY
'@; $script | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 bash -s","timeout_ms":600000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

payload.message:
Two concrete results came out of this pass.

On `edgesynology1`, the `Disney` storm is definitely in the `styleguides` share root:
- `view 18` maps to `@styleguides` watching `/volume1/styleguides`
- I counted `17,545` `Disney` hits on `view 18`
- the Drive admin DB row is:
  - `name=@styleguides`
  - `watch_path=/volume1/styleguides`

I also confirmed this is not just NAS-to-NAS churn. The `styleguides` logs on `edgesynology1` contain workstation-style temp activity such as:
- `Unconfirmed *.crdownload` scrubbing events in `view 18`
- that is browser-download temp file behavior
- so at least some of the churn is being introduced by a human workstation touching files inside `/volume1/styleguides`, then propagated onward by legitimate ShareSync

What I could and could not attribute:
- I can prove the churn is upstream on `edgesynology1`
- I can prove it is in `styleguides`, not caused by the `edgesynology2` ShareSync link itself
- I cannot yet tie `view 18` to one exact user/device from the server DB alone, because `@styleguides` is a shared-root watch and it does not have its own logged-in client session row
- the session DB only shows currently connected human clients in general, for example:
  - `ALBT16` as `ahazan`
  - `nathalies-MacBook-Air.local` as `IML\NSchuchman`
  - `PEREGRINE` as `IML\MCameron`
  - `DERRICK-LAPTOP` as `IML\Dsmith`
  - `SANGEL` as `IML\asilva`
  - `ZAR-LAPTOP` as `IML\zcalderon`
- but it does not say “this session wrote `/Disney/...`”

On the `homes` side, I got a sharper answer too:
- on `edgesynology1`, the home-backed Drive watch is only `/volume1/homes/ahazan/Drive` as `view 10`
- not the whole `/volume1/homes/ahazan`
- on `edgesynology2`, earlier evidence showed `My Drive`/home-backed watching, but this pass did not reveal another obvious package dependency besides Drive/home-related behavior itself
- so the main thing likely blocking `User Home Service` disablement is still Synology Drive `My Drive` or another home-backed feature, not ShareSync

My current best conclusion:
- `styleguides/Disney` churn source is upstream workstation activity on `edgesynology1`
- the cleanest proven symptom is `.crdownload` inside watched `styleguides`
- `homes` risk looks narrower than before because at least on `edgesynology1` the watched path is specifically `.../ahazan/Drive`

Next highest-value read-only step:
- inspect Drive admin / audit surfaces on `edgesynology1` for per-file actor history, if available
- otherwise use OS/file timestamps plus narrower log windows around specific `Disney` paths to infer which active user/device was working there

If you want, I’ll keep going on `edgesynology1` and try to extract per-file actor attribution for a specific hot `Disney` subtree.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

Use the `request_user_input` tool only when it is listed in the available tools for this turn.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text:
I'm Albert, owner of POP Creations (wholesale licensed home decor). I have a Synology NAS called edgesynology1 at 192.168.3.100 (SSH port 22, user `ahazan`, key `C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien` / `916-alien.ppk`).

You have these MCP tools available:
- **synology-monitor MCP** — `run_command` tool for READ-ONLY NAS shell commands
- **Windows-MCP** — PowerShell tool on my Windows workstation. Use plink for SSH to the NAS (see execution notes below). Always pass an explicit `timeout` value — default is 30s which is too low for most operations; use 120 for quick commands, 600–900 for anything that scans large directories.

---

## Background: what was already done in /volume1/styleguides

A prior session investigated and cleaned up Synology Drive conflict file explosion in `/volume1/styleguides`. Files had `(1)` appended by Synology Drive's conflict resolution, creating chains like `filename (1) (1) (1).psd`. The cleanup was:
- **Tier 1** (multi-`(1)` chains — `* (1) (1)*`): All had originals present, all byte-for-byte identical. Bulk deleted 7,818 files, ~86 GB freed. Complete.
- **Tier 2** (single `(1)` files — `* (1).*`): Checked each for a matching original. Deleted 322 that had originals; kept 90 orphans (no original existed — they were the only copy). Complete.

The key insight from styleguides: **multi-`(1)` chains were always safe to bulk delete. Single `(1)` files required per-file checking.**

---

## Your task: investigate ALL other folders on volume1

The following folders on edgesynology1 volume1 contain `(1)` files that have NOT been cleaned up yet. A preliminary scan found:

| Folder | Total `(1)` files | Multi-`(1)` chains | Single `(1)` files | Orphans (no original) |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| /volume1/mac | 7,875 | 0 | 7,875 | 7,586 |
| /volume1/files | 1,411 | 23 | 1,348 | 1,000 |
| /volume1/Photography | 745 | 0 | 738 | 698 |
| /volume1/users | 672 | 7 | 649 | 320 |
| /volume1/mgmt | 205 | 1 | 199 | 139 |
| /volume1/Coldlion | 93 | 0 | 93 | 45 |
| /volume1/freelancers | 76 | 0 | 76 | 73 |
| /volume1/oldStyleguides | 35 | 0 | 35 | 7 |

**Total disk used by all `(1)` files across these folders: ~8.7 GB**

---

## CRITICAL WARNING — these folders are different from /styleguides

A sample inspection of `/volume1/mac` orphans revealed that many `(1)` files there do NOT appear to be Synology Drive conflicts. Examples found:
- Folders containing `filename (1).jpg`, `filename (2).jpg`, `filename (3).jpg` with NO plain `filename.jpg` — suggesting `(1)`, `(2)`, `(3)` was intentional version numbering, not conflict copies
- `(1)` files existing alongside completely different filenames (no matching base name at all)

This means **you cannot assume these are safe to delete without investigation**. The orphan rate in `/volume1/mac` is 96% (7,586 of 7,875) — almost all `(1)` files have no matching original. In `/styleguides` the orphan rate was only ~22%.

**Before recommending or executing any deletions, you must:**
1. Understand what each folder is used for
2. Determine whether `(1)` naming in that folder is Synology Drive conflicts OR intentional versioning
3. Sample-inspect the actual files before concluding anything is safe to delete

---

## What these folders contain (business context)

- `/volume1/mac` — main product asset library. Product photos, PSD files, artwork. Very large, business-critical. Sample inspection showed deliberate `(1)`, `(2)`, `(3)` version sequences — treat with extreme caution.
- `/volume1/files` — general file storage, mixed content
- `/volume1/Photography` — product photography
- `/volume1/users` — user home-like folders
- `/volume1/mgmt` — management/admin files
- `/volume1/Coldlion` — ERP/operations-related files
- `/volume1/freelancers` — files shared with/from freelancers
- `/volume1/oldStyleguides` — archived older styleguides

---

## Investigation approach

For each folder, determine:
1. **What is the `(1)` naming pattern?** — Synology Drive conflicts (duplicate of an existing file) vs. intentional versioning (sequential `(1)`, `(2)`, `(3)` with no plain original)
2. **What is the actual safe-to-delete count?** — Only files where an identical original exists AND the `(1)` copy is redundant
3. **What are the orphans actually?** — Unique files that must be kept vs. intentional versions that may or may not be needed

For `/volume1/mac` specifically: given the 96% orphan rate and evidence of intentional versioning, the working hypothesis is that most or all `(1)` files there should be kept. Confirm or disprove this with sampling before recommending any action.

---

## Execution infrastructure (how SSH to the NAS works)

**synology-monitor `run_command`** is read-only — use it for all investigation/scanning. It directly SSH's into the NAS. Good for `find`, `ls`, `wc -l`, `du`, sampling files, etc.

**For write operations** (when you eventually need to delete confirmed-safe files): use Windows-MCP PowerShell + plink. The pattern that works:

Step 1 — Write script to NAS via plink stdin (fast, returns immediately):
```powershell
$script = @'
#!/bin/bash
# ... script content ...
'@
```

`/volume1/mac` is not a general Synology Drive-conflict cleanup candidate. | `/volume1/oldStyleguides` | Not like `/styleguides`; most original-matching files differ | 3 files | 28.6 MB | Do not assume old styleguides are safe. Whitelist only. |

Confirmed safe total across all investigated folders: `508` files, about `6.86 GB`.

A few high-signal examples:

- `/volume1/mac` also had exact-name predecessors that were different content, for example `MBX25SAR2 (1).psd` vs `MBX25SAR2.psd`.

Recommendation:

- `Do not use wildcard deletion` in any folder other than a manifest built from the exact identical list.

what's a a whitelist-only cleanup?

A whitelist-only cleanup means I delete only files that were explicitly proven safe one by one, instead of deleting by a broad filename pattern.

In this case, a `/volume1/users` file was deleted only if all of these were true:
- its name ended in ` (1)` exactly once
- the same folder also had the plain predecessor filename
- both files had the same size
- both files had the same hash, so they were byte-for-byte identical

Anything that failed any check was kept. That includes:
- `(1)` files with no plain original
- `(1)` files whose plain original existed but had different content
- numbered sets like `(1)`, `(2)`, `(3)` that looked intentional
- case-only oddities unless specifically proven safe

So “whitelist” here means the script built an implicit allowed-to-delete list from verified duplicates, rather than trusting a wildcard like `* (1).*`.

we don't use homes folders. i think it was required for one or more users by something else i was doing or had installed on the synology. but can we safely stop it from using the device's resources?

/volume1/mac
/volume1/mgmt
/volume1/Coldlion
/volume1/files
/volume1/Photography
/volume1/styleguides
/volume1/users
are all valid active folders and are not overly broad, we need those synced. so how do we reduce shared-root churn that doesn't affect actual legitimate business use?

Yes, probably.

The safe boundary is:

- `Do not` disable DSM’s global `User Home Service` yet.
- `Do` stop Synology Drive from watching/indexing `My Drive` home folders if you do not use them.

Why:
- Your live logs show `/volume1/homes/...` is coming from Synology Drive `My Drive`, not ShareSync.
- Your live ShareSync sessions on `edgesynology2` are only for:
  - `/volume1/mac`
  - `/volume1/mgmt`
  - `/volume1/Coldlion`
  - `/volume1/files`
  - `/volume1/Photography`
  - `/volume1/styleguides`
  - `/volume1/users`
- `My Drive` watches were added separately, for example `ahazan <-> /volume1/homes/ahazan` and `popdam <-> /volume1/homes/popdam`.

So if homes may be needed by some other package, the low-risk move is:
- leave `homes` itself alone
- disable Synology Drive `My Drive` for unused users, or globally if nobody uses it
- unlink stale Drive clients that only exist for personal/home sync

That should reduce Drive indexing/watch load without touching your valid NAS-to-NAS share sync set.

For the shared-root churn, the main issue is not that those roots are too broad. The issue is too many write-capable sync actors touching the same trees.

The practical way to reduce churn without hurting real business use is:

1. Keep ShareSync between the two NASes for the seven valid roots.
2. Minimize desktop Synology Drive client sync on those same roots, especially bidirectional sync.
3. Identify and unlink stale or unnecessary Drive clients first.
4. For users who need access, prefer:
- SMB/File Station/Web access for large shared creative folders
- selective sync for narrow working subfolders
- one-way backup tasks instead of two-way sync where possible
5. Stop personal `My Drive` watches if unused.
6. Exclude obviously useless churn sources where possible:
- recycle bins
- temp/swap files
- Adobe/cache/scratch-style folders if they are inside synced trees
7. On the worst hotspots like `styleguides/Disney`, find the specific device/account causing repeated rescans/conflicts and remove that client before changing the whole sync architecture.

The pattern from your logs supports that:
- ShareSync is syncing the seven valid roots NAS-to-NAS.
- Synology Drive is also watching those same roots locally.
- Additional `My Drive` watches exist for home folders.
- Historical personal/computer content inside valid shares is adding noise.
- The duplicate storms are most consistent with client-side sync conflicts layered on top of legitimate shared-root sync.

My recommendation:
- First disable Synology Drive `My Drive` for homes if you truly do not use it.
- Then audit and unlink stale desktop/mobile Drive clients.
- Only after that, if churn is still high, narrow specific client sync tasks on the hottest roots rather than changing the NAS-to-NAS ShareSync set.

If you want, I can do the next read-only pass to produce a concrete action list:
- which `My Drive` users are active on `edgesynology2`
- which Drive clients/devices are still connected
- which client/account appears to be driving the most churn in `styleguides`, `mac`, and `files`

how do i stop Synology Drive from watching/indexing My Drive home folders?
how do i disable Synology Drive My Drive for unused users?
how do i Minimize desktop Synology Drive client sync for remote employees that are legitimately working on these files/folders?
how do i Exclude obviously useless churn sources?
can you find the specific device/account causing repeated rescans/conflicts in styleguides/Disney?

Short version: yes, you can probably stop `My Drive` from consuming resources without touching the seven valid ShareSync roots.

What your logs show is:

- `homes` usage is coming from Synology Drive `My Drive`, not ShareSync.
- The `Disney` rescan storm I can prove right now is running inside ShareSync `session 26` for `/volume1/styleguides`, connected to `192.168.3.100` as `ahazan`, not clearly from a named employee laptop.

**1. Stop Synology Drive from watching/indexing My Drive home folders**
Lowest-risk path:

- In `Synology Drive Admin Console`, go to the area where `Team Folder` and `My Drive (home)` are managed.
- Disable `My Drive (home)` if nobody uses personal Drive space.
- If the UI only lets you do it per-user, disable it for all users you do not want.

Why I’m confident this is the right layer:
- Official Synology docs distinguish `My Drive (home)` from `homes`; `My Drive` is the personal home-backed Drive space, while `homes` is tied to DSM user home service. [Synology KB: My Drive vs homes](https://kb.synology.com/en-me/DSM/tutorial/Drive_difference_between_homes_My_Drive_home_folders)
- Official DSM docs show `homes` itself belongs to the DSM `User Home Service`, which is a separate system feature. [Synology KB: User Home Service](https://kb.synology.com/en-us/DSM/help/DSM/AdminCenter/file_user_advanced)

So:
- `Disable My Drive first`
- `Do not disable DSM User Home Service yet` unless you know no package still depends on it

**2. Disable Synology Drive My Drive for unused users**
Use the same Drive Admin Console `My Drive` management area and turn it off for users who should only use team folders.

From your own logs, `My Drive` was enabled at least for:
- `ahazan`
- `popdam`
- and historically domain users like `IML\MCameron`, `IML\adweck`, `IML\lherrera`, `IML\apinilla`, `IML\asilva`, `IML\eparkin`

So the next cleanup target is:
- disable `My Drive` for any of those who do not need personal Drive space
- unlink stale Drive clients for ex-employees or old devices

**3. Minimize desktop Synology Drive client sync for legitimate remote workers**
Do not sync the full seven shares to desktops unless there is a very specific reason.

Best-practice version for your environment:
- Give each remote worker only the smallest subfolder set they actively need.
- Avoid two-way sync on broad creative trees like large `styleguides` branches.
- Prefer SMB/VPN, remote desktop, or browser/File Station access for heavy shared asset libraries.
- Use one-way backup tasks when the workstation is a source archive, not a collaboration node.
- Avoid having multiple users sync the same live PSD/AI-heavy folder tree to local disks.
- If a user truly needs Drive Client, use selective sync down to project/customer/licensor subfolders, not whole departments.

For your setup, the high-risk pattern is:
- `ShareSync between NASes` plus `multiple desktop two-way Drive clients on the same live folders`

That combination creates exactly the churn you’re seeing.

**4. Exclude obviously useless churn sources**
You should blacklist temp/junk that should never traverse Drive.

High-value exclusions for your environment:
- Adobe temp files like `~ai-*.tmp`
- generic `*.tmp`, `*.temp`, `*.swp`, `*.lnk`
- macOS junk like `__MACOSX`
- working cache/scratch folders
- app-generated temp staging folders
- recycle-bin noise if that path is exposed through sync workflows

I can already prove some filtering is happening:
- your logs show `~ai-..._.tmp` files being blocked by suffix-rule filters on `2026-05-04`

So the next move is not “invent filtering,” it’s “expand and verify the blacklist.”

**5. Can I find the specific device/account causing repeated rescans/conflicts in `styleguides/Disney`?**
Partially, yes.

What I can prove right now:
- The `Disney` storm is in `view 5` / `styleguides`
- The repeated directory rescans are showing in `ShareSync` daemon logs, not just generic Drive-client logs
- On `2026-05-04`, ShareSync `session 26` was started for:
  - `share_name: "styleguides"`
  - `sync_folder: "/volume1/styleguides"`
- The active ShareSync connection is to:
  - `server_ip: 192.168.3.100`
  - `username: ahazan`
  - `computer_name: edgesynology2`

What I cannot prove from the current logs:
- a specific employee laptop name as the original source of the Disney edits
- whether the original churn started on `edgesynology1` directly, or on a workstation syncing into `edgesynology1`

So the strongest current attribution is:
- `Immediate actor`: ShareSync session 26 on `edgesynology2`
- `Remote peer`: `edgesynology1` at `192.168.3.100`
- `Unproven upstream origin`: a specific user device

That means the Disney storm is currently propagating at the NAS-to-NAS layer, even if a workstation originally caused it.

**Recommendation order**
1. Disable unused `My Drive` users first.
2. Keep the seven valid ShareSync roots unchanged.
3. Audit desktop Drive clients and remove broad two-way sync tasks on those roots.
4. Expand suffix/path exclusions.
5. Then do a deeper attribution pass on `edgesynology1` to find which workstation or sync task is feeding `styleguides/Disney`.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

## User Profile

The user uses Codex for two recurring modes of work: deep Synology NAS troubleshooting around `\\?\C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project`, and direct Codex-product/configuration tasks on Windows. They expect the agent to execute when they ask for a fix or config change, but to stay read-only when they explicitly say investigation only.

They care about root cause and concrete proof. On infrastructure and hardware questions, they push back on speculation, especially costly recommendations such as replacing a 20TB drive. They prefer exact paths, exact error strings, and a clear blocked-vs-usable status instead of vague progress language.

They also correct product-surface assumptions quickly. If a settings path or UI label does not match what they actually see in Codex, they expect the answer to be updated to the current surface rather than defended with older ChatGPT wording. When they provide an exact config stanza or operational target, they generally want it implemented directly with minimal ceremony.

## User preferences

- When they ask "can you fix it?", make the actual code or config change and carry it through verification or deployment instead of stopping at diagnosis.
- When they ask "try again" or "does it now have everything you need?", answer with a precise blocked-vs-usable status.
- When they say "do investigation only right now. don't make any changes", keep the workflow read-only until they explicitly approve remediation.
- For Synology investigations, follow the correction "call get_investigation_guide first before determining that you don't have snapshot tools" as a default.
- If the built-in `mcp__synology_monitor__` path stalls, pivot to direct HTTP MCP when available instead of repeatedly retrying the same route.
- When diagnosing "missing files", treat "investigate why" as a root-cause request, not a simple existence/status check.
- When the user says a hardware recommendation is "just on a hunch", gather comparative proof before suggesting replacement.
- When they say "indexing should not make a NAS unusable", keep tracing below the app layer; do not settle for a shallow service-level explanation.
- When they say "can you do it for me?" after an operational explanation, prefer low-risk direct action over handing them service names or shell steps.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message: I have the log schema now. The useful table is `log_table`, and it already stores `username`, `ip_address`, `client_type`, and five string/path fields per event. I’m querying that next for `Disney`, `Winnie the Pooh`, `Stitch`, and `Cars` within `view 18`.

content.text: I have the log schema now. The useful table is `log_table`, and it already stores `username`, `ip_address`, `client_type`, and five string/path fields per event. I’m querying that next for `Disney`, `Winnie the Pooh`, `Stitch`, and `Cars` within `view 18`.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$py = @'
import sqlite3, datetime
conn = sqlite3.connect('/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/log-db.sqlite')
cur = conn.cursor()
needle = '%Disney%'
q = """
SELECT COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type, COUNT(*)
FROM log_table
WHERE view_id=18 AND (
  COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE ? OR
  COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE ? OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE ?
)
GROUP BY COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type
ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC
LIMIT 30
"""
for row in cur.execute(q, [needle]*10):
    print('\t'.join(str(x) for x in row))
'@; $py | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 "python3 -"","timeout_ms":120000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

payload.arguments:
{"command":"$py = @'
import sqlite3
conn = sqlite3.connect('/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/log-db.sqlite')
cur = conn.cursor()
q = """
SELECT date(time,'unixepoch','localtime') AS day,
       COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(s2,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type, COUNT(*)
FROM log_table
WHERE view_id=18 AND (
  COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR
  COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Disney%'
)
GROUP BY day, COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(s2,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type
ORDER BY day DESC, COUNT(*) DESC
LIMIT 80
"""
for row in cur.execute(q):
    print('\t'.join(str(x) for x in row))
'@; $py | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 "python3 -"","timeout_ms":120000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

{"command":"$py = @'
import sqlite3
conn = sqlite3.connect('/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/log-db.sqlite')
cur = conn.cursor()
q = """
SELECT COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(s2,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type,
       SUM(CASE WHEN COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS pooh,
       SUM(CASE WHEN COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS stitch,
       SUM(CASE WHEN COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Cars%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cars,
       COUNT(*) as total
FROM log_table
WHERE view_id=18 AND (
  COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR
  COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Disney%'
)
GROUP BY COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(s2,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type
ORDER BY total DESC
LIMIT 25
"""
for row in cur.execute(q):
    print('\t'.join(str(x) for x in row))
'@; $py | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 "python3 -"","timeout_ms":120000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

command:
$py = @'
import sqlite3
conn = sqlite3.connect('/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/log-db.sqlite')
cur = conn.cursor()
q = """
SELECT COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(s2,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type,
       SUM(CASE WHEN COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Winnie the Pooh%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS pooh,
       SUM(CASE WHEN COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Stitch%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS stitch,
       SUM(CASE WHEN COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Cars%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Cars%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cars,
       COUNT(*) as total
FROM log_table
WHERE view_id=18 AND (
  COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR
  COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%Disney%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%Disney%'
)
GROUP BY COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(s2,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type
ORDER BY total DESC
LIMIT 25
"""
for row in cur.execute(q):
    print('\t'.join(str(x) for x in row))

payload.message: I now have a likely upstream actor, but I want one more corroboration point from the temp-file side. I’m checking whether the `Unconfirmed *.crdownload` and similar workstation-temp activity in `styleguides` lines up with the same account/device, rather than just normal file uploads.

content.text: I now have a likely upstream actor, but I want one more corroboration point from the temp-file side. I’m checking whether the `Unconfirmed *.crdownload` and similar workstation-temp activity in `styleguides` lines up with the same account/device, rather than just normal file uploads.

payload.name: shell_command
payload.arguments:
{"command":"$py = @'
import sqlite3
conn = sqlite3.connect('/volume1/@synologydrive/@sync/log-db.sqlite')
cur = conn.cursor()
q = """
SELECT datetime(time,'unixepoch','localtime') AS ts, type, COALESCE(username,''), COALESCE(s2,''), COALESCE(ip_address,''), client_type,
       COALESCE(s1,''), COALESCE(p1,'')
FROM log_table
WHERE view_id=18 AND (
  COALESCE(s1,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(s2,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(s3,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(s4,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(s5,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR
  COALESCE(p1,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(p2,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(p3,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(p4,'') LIKE '%crdownload%' OR COALESCE(p5,'') LIKE '%crdownload%'
)
ORDER BY time DESC
LIMIT 40
"""
for row in cur.execute(q):
    print(' | '.join(str(x) for x in row))
'@; $py | & 'C:\Program Files\PuTTY\plink.exe' -batch -i 'C:\Users\ahazan2\.ssh\916-alien.ppk' -P 22 -hostkey 'SHA256:JPqadQuh+QWEAr5oUT0OF4hY/LdmWXOtTs2FGXGo5QI' ahazan@192.168.3.100 "python3 -"","timeout_ms":120000,"workdir":"C:\Users\ahazan2\Documents\New project"}

payload.message:
I can now name a specific upstream workstation behind a large share of the `styleguides/Disney` churn on `edgesynology1`.

The strongest human-origin source I found is `IML\LArevalo` on device `LAREVALO1603`. In Synology Drive’s `log-db.sqlite` for `view 18` (`/volume1/styleguides`), that device accounts for `2,188` Disney-tagged events overall and `1,548` since `2026-04-28`, far more than any other non-ShareSync user. A concrete example is `2026-05-01 11:57:58` to `11:58:18`, where `LAREVALO1603` wrote a batch under `/Disney/Princess/2027/27 JAN 5135075-multi-princess--fairytale-dreams---style-guide/...`; the same exact paths were then mirrored a few minutes later to `edgesynology2` as `ahazan` from `192.168.3.101`. That proves the upstream write was the workstation, not the NAS-to-NAS ShareSync leg.

There is a second, smaller human source: `IML\dmoreno` on `DESKTOP-FFV7J81`, with `51` Disney events since `2026-04-28`, concentrated in `/Disney/Winnie the Pooh/...` on `2026-04-29`. So `Disney` is not being touched by only one person, but `LArevalo1603` is the dominant current human actor I can prove from the logs.

I also tied the workstation-temp churn to the same user. The `Unconfirmed *.crdownload` activity inside watched styleguide roots is coming from `IML\LArevalo` on `LAREVALO1603`, with mirrored follow-on events to `edgesynology3` and `edgesynology2`. The examples I captured were under `Aardman Animations` rather than `Disney`, but they prove this device is actively generating the kind of browser-download temp noise that causes rescans and conflict churn inside synced shared roots.

What this means:
- `edgesynology2` is not the root cause for `Disney`; it is replaying legitimate ShareSync traffic from `edgesynology1`.
- The best-proven upstream workstation to audit first is `LAREVALO1603`.
- A second workstation worth checking is `DESKTOP-FFV7J81`.
- The churn pattern is consistent with people downloading, unpacking, and working directly inside live synced styleguide folders.

The highest-value next step is operational, not architectural:
- audit Synology Drive Client on `LAREVALO1603`
- check whether that user is syncing a broad `styleguides` subtree locally instead of using a narrower working folder
- stop browser downloads, zip extraction, and temp-file-heavy work directly inside the live synced `styleguides` tree

No changes were made.
